task: I’m trying to get a small property sale back on track after the title company kicked back a few items and the county wants a revised filing. Can you help me figure out the cleanest order to handle the paperwork so I don’t lose time or create another round of corrections?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest order is usually:

1. **Fix the county filing issue first**
   - Get the exact rejection comments.
   - Identify whether it’s a legal description, vesting/entity name, recording format, fees, or missing attachment.
   - Correct that before anything else, because title will usually mirror the recording package.

2. **Update the title company on the corrected version**
   - Send the revised filing docs and ask them to confirm the title commitment / closing package will match.
   - Make sure the vesting, legal description, and any entity names are identical across documents.

3. **Clear the title company’s kicked-back items one by one**
   - Treat each as a separate fix, not one blended response.
   - Ask for a written list of exactly what they need, with no “etc.” items.

4. **Reconcile the settlement figures**
   - Confirm prorations, payoffs, credits, and any holdbacks are tied to the corrected filing.
   - Don’t let the numbers finalize until the paperwork version is locked.

5. **Reissue the closing set**
   - Once county and title are aligned, circulate the final docs for signature.
   - Then do a final check before funding/recording.

A good rule: **county filing first, title second, money third, signatures last** — unless the title company says a specific item is a funding blocker.

If you want, I can turn this into a **tight same-day action checklist** with:
- who to contact first
- what to ask each party
- what has to match exactly
- what items usually cause a second rejection

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close, but it’s a little too generic. I need the order by leverage, not just a nice sequence.

What matters is: what is the actual blocker — county rejection code, title exception, or lender/funding condition? If the county filing is what’s holding recording, fix that first and don’t touch the rest until the revised version is clean. If title is just waiting on a match to the county doc, then get the county correction drafted, send that exact version to title, and force them to confirm in writing that their package will mirror it.

I’d want the steps broken out like this:
- exact county rejection reason
- exact title kickback items, separately
- which item controls recording
- which item controls funding
- what has to be identical across every document

Also, don’t assume the title company’s list is correct. I’ve seen them kick back things that were really just mismatches in legal description or vesting language. I’d rather have a clean matrix: document, version, issue, who fixes it, who signs off.

If you want to make this actually useful, give me the same-day checklist in that format. Not a broad outline.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the same-day version, ordered by **leverage / blocker**, not by category.

## 1) Identify the controlling blocker first
Ask these in order:

1. **What exactly did the county reject?**
   - rejection code
   - stamped comments
   - whether it’s recording format, legal description, vesting/entity name, fee issue, missing attachment, or signature issue

2. **What exactly did title kick back?**
   - ask for each item separately
   - don’t accept a blended list

3. **What is blocking recording vs funding?**
   - recording blocker: county rejection / document mismatch
   - funding blocker: lender/title condition, payoff, entity mismatch, missing signed docs

If you don’t know which one controls the chain, you can waste a day fixing the wrong thing.

---

## 2) Fix the county item that controls recording
If the county is the blocker, do **that** first and do not rework the rest until the revised version is clean.

### County-first rule:
- revise the exact rejected item
- keep every other document frozen
- make sure the corrected version is the one title will mirror

### What must match exactly:
- legal description
- vesting / grantee name
- entity name and authority
- doc title / exhibit references
- notary / signature block format if that was the issue

---

## 3) Send the exact revised county version to title
Before title reissues anything, send them the corrected county draft and require written confirmation that:

- their package will **mirror the county version**
- no other language changes are being introduced
- vesting, legal description, and entity name are identical across all docs

This is where you stop the “fix one thing, create two new mismatches” problem.

---

## 4) Clear title kickbacks one by one
Treat each title issue separately.

### For each title kickback, ask:
- what document is affected
- what exact language is wrong
- whether it is a real defect or just a mismatch
- who is responsible for the correction
- whether title will sign off once corrected

A lot of title kickbacks are really just:
- legal description mismatch
- vesting mismatch
- entity name mismatch
- missing exhibit
- stale signature page
- payoff / lien / exception issue

---

## 5) Confirm what controls funding
Separate **recording** from **funding**.

### Funding blockers usually include:
- lender conditions
- title exceptions not cleared
- payoff statement missing or expired
- entity authority not documented
- final signed docs not complete
- settlement figures not reconciled

If funding is blocked, don’t assume county cleanup alone will solve it.

---

## 6) Lock the “identical across every document” items
These are the items that need to be the same everywhere:

- legal description
- vesting / grantee / borrower name
- entity name
- signer name and authority
- property address if used
- exhibit references
- recording reference numbers
- payoff / settlement figures if already finalized

If any one of those differs, expect another round of corrections.

---

# Same-day working matrix

| Document | Version | Issue | Who fixes it | Who signs off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| County filing | Rejected version | County rejection reason | Prep counsel / title / filing agent | County recorder / filing desk |
| County filing | Revised version | Must cure exact rejection | Same as above | County recorder |
| Title commitment / closing package | Current draft | Must mirror county version | Title company | Title officer |
| Settlement statement | Current draft | Must match corrected docs and payoffs | Escrow / title | Title officer / lender if needed |
| Vesting / signature docs | Current draft | Name / authority mismatch | Counsel / signer | Title + signer |
| Legal description exhibit | Current draft | Description mismatch | Title / survey / counsel | Title officer |

---

# Fast order of operations
1. Get county rejection reason in writing.
2. Identify whether county or title controls recording.
3. Correct the county item first if it’s the blocker.
4. Send the exact corrected version to title.
5. Force title to confirm in writing they will mirror it.
6. Clear title kickbacks one by one.
7. Reconcile settlement / payoff / funding conditions.
8. Reissue the final set only after the text is locked.

If you want, I can turn this into a **fillable checklist** you can send to the title officer today, with blanks for:
- county rejection reason
- title issue
- blocker type
- responsible party
- sign-off status

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still missing one piece: the actual decision tree when county and title are pointing at each other and nobody wants to own the fix.

I want it tighter — like:

- if county rejection is purely recording-format, fix county and freeze title except for mirror review
- if title exception is based on the bad filing, fix county first, then reissue title
- if lender/funding is the hard stop, do not waste time polishing county language until the funding condition is identified in writing

And I want the matrix to separate:
- blocker
- dependency
- can this wait until after recording?
- who has the pen
- who can actually stop closing

That’s the leverage part. Otherwise it’s just a checklist with prettier spacing.

Give me the fill-in version, but make it operational — something I can use to pin down the title officer in one call.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the **operational decision tree + fill-in matrix** version.

## 1) Decision tree: who owns the fix?

### A. If the county rejection is **purely recording-format**
Examples: margin issue, font/scan issue, missing cover page, wrong fee, bad acknowledgment format.

- **Fix the county filing first**
- **Freeze title** except for mirror review
- Title should only confirm: “our package matches the corrected county version”
- Do **not** let title introduce new edits unless they are required to mirror the county cure

**Rule:** county cures the filing; title mirrors it.

---

### B. If the title exception is caused by the bad filing
Examples: legal description mismatch, vesting mismatch, entity name mismatch, exhibit mismatch.

- **Fix the county version first**
- Send that exact revised version to title
- Title then reissues their package to match it
- Force title to confirm in writing that the exception will clear on the revised filing

**Rule:** the bad filing is the source; title should not be “fixing” around it.

---

### C. If lender/funding is the hard stop
Examples: stale payoff, missing authority, unsigned docs, unresolved lender condition, funding approval not issued.

- **Do not waste time polishing county language**
- Identify the funding condition in writing first
- Fix only what is necessary to satisfy funding
- County/title cleanup can wait unless it directly affects funding or recording

**Rule:** funding condition controls the schedule if it is the actual stop.

---

## 2) One-call leverage questions for the title officer
Ask these in order:

1. **What is the exact blocker right now: county recording, title exception, or lender/funding condition?**
2. **Which item actually stops closing today?**
3. **Which item can wait until after recording?**
4. **What exact document version do you need mirrored?**
5. **What language must be identical across all docs?**
6. **Who has the pen on the fix?**
7. **Who can actually stop closing if this is not resolved?**

If they can’t answer #1 and #2 cleanly, they’re probably blending issues.

---

## 3) Fill-in matrix

| Blocker | Dependency | Can this wait until after recording? | Who has the pen? | Who can actually stop closing? |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| County rejection: [fill in exact code/reason] | [e.g. corrected legal description / acknowledgment / fee / exhibit] | Yes / No | [county filing agent / title / counsel] | [county recorder / title officer / lender] |
| Title exception: [fill in exact exception] | [e.g. revised county filing / corrected vesting / payoff] | Yes / No | [title officer / counsel / escrow] | [title officer / underwriter / lender] |
| Lender condition: [fill in exact condition] | [e.g. signed docs / updated payoff / authority] | Yes / No | [lender / borrower / counsel] | [lender / funding desk] |
| Settlement mismatch: [fill in exact item] | [e.g. prorations / credits / payoff] | Yes / No | [escrow / title] | [lender / title officer] |
| Entity / vesting mismatch: [fill in exact mismatch] | [e.g. corrected signature block / resolution] | Yes / No | [counsel / signer / title] | [title officer / lender] |

---

## 4) What to lock down in writing
Before anyone reissues anything, get written confirmation on:

- exact county rejection reason
- exact title kickback items, separately listed
- whether title is mirroring the county cure or adding independent changes
- whether the issue blocks **recording**, **funding**, or both
- which version is the controlling draft

---

## 5) Short script for the title officer
You can use this verbatim:

> I need the controlling blocker identified in writing: county recording, title exception, or lender/funding condition.  
> If it’s county-only, we fix county and title mirrors it.  
> If title is based on the bad filing, we correct the county version first and reissue title to match.  
> If funding is the hard stop, tell me the exact funding condition now so we don’t waste time polishing non-controlling items.  
> Please send me the exact document version, the exact mismatch, who has the pen, and who can actually stop closing.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page call sheet** with blanks you can literally read off to the title officer.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the shape of it. Tight enough to use.

The only thing I’d want added is a cleaner distinction between **who can stall** and **who actually controls the fix** — those are not always the same. But this is usable as-is. I can pin a title officer with that script.
